coredata.dc:title: Activation of persulfate by core–shell structured Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag nanocomposite for the efficient degradation of penicillin
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yang
Zhang, Bo-Tao
Teng, Yanguo
Zhao, Juanjuan
Kuang, Lulu
Sun, Xiaojie
coredata.dc:description: A core–shell structured catalyst (Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag) for persulfate activation and efficient oxidation of penicillin was synthesized using a hydrothermal method and chemical reduction. Fe3O4 nanoparticles with a diameter of 5–20 nm composed the multi-cores which encapsulated by amorphous carbon and 3–10 nm carbon dots while Ag nanoparticles (5–10 nm) were formed on the shell. Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag had a specific surface area of 26.01 m2/g and a saturation magnetization of 21.29 emu/g. Penicillin was removed in short time by Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated persulfate system, which could be attributed to the efficient synergy of Fe3O4, carbon dots, and Ag in the core–shell structured catalyst. Ag nanoparticles and carbon dots were served as vehicles and active sites, respectively, and the system could generate free radicals efficiently and continuously. Further, the Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag showed stability and minimal Fe/Ag leakage. The radicals were identified by electron spin resonance spectroscopy and the radical contributions were determined via scavenger experiments. The degradation pathways of penicillin were elucidated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Persulfate
Core-shell structure
Nano particles
Catalytic oxidation
Penicillin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reactive species and possible activation mechanisms

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Fe3O4@C/CDs (a), Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag (b-d), and element mappings (e) of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag.
Raman spectra (a), FTIR spectra (b), and XRD patterns (c) of Fe3O4, CDs, Ag NPs, Fe3O4@C/CDs, and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag.
Survey scan (a), O 1s (b), C 1s (c), Ag 3d (d), Fe 2p (e), and Fe 3p (f) XPS spectra of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag.
Up-converted photoluminescence spectra (a) of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (b) of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C/CDs, and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag.
Removal profiles in different systems. Penicillin, 50 μM; PS, 5 mM; Catalyst, 0.2 g/L.
Effect of catalyst dosage (a), oxidant concentration (b), penicillin concentration (c) and pH (d) on degradation of penicillin. Penicillin, 1–100 μM; PS, 0–5 mM; Catalyst, 0–0.5 g/L; pH 3–11.

Ln(C/C0) versus time for k calculation by pseudo-first-order kinetic model and Arrhenius plot of penicillin degradation in Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system (a). Reusability of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag for penicillin degradation (b). Penicillin, 50 μM; PS, 5 mM; Catalyst, 0.2 g/L.

ESR spectra of DMPO·–OH, DMPO·-SO4 -, DMPOX (a), DMPO-·O2 – (b) and TEMPO (c) in PS/ Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag system. Effect of scavenger reagents on penicillin oxidative degradation (d). DMPO, 100 mM; TEMP, 100 mM; persulfate, 1 mM; Catalyst, 0.2 g/L, Tert-butyl alcohol, 500 mM; Methanol, 500 mM; p-BQ, 500 mM; NaN3, 500 mM.
The mechanism of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system for PEN degradation.

head.ce:title: Activation of persulfate by core–shell structured Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag nanocomposite for the efficient degradation of penicillin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Water Sciences, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Water Sciences, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Groundwater Pollution Control and Remediation, Ministry of Education, Beijing 100875, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Groundwater Pollution Control and Remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Groundwater Pollution Control and Remediation, Ministry of Education, Beijing 100875, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Water Sciences, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A core–shell structured catalyst (Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag) for persulfate activation and efficient oxidation of penicillin was synthesized using a hydrothermal method and chemical reduction. Fe3O4 nanoparticles with a diameter of 5–20 nm composed the multi-cores which encapsulated by amorphous carbon and 3–10 nm carbon dots while Ag nanoparticles (5–10 nm) were formed on the shell. Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag had a specific surface area of 26.01 m2/g and a saturation magnetization of 21.29 emu/g. Penicillin was removed in short time by Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated persulfate system, which could be attributed to the efficient synergy of Fe3O4, carbon dots, and Ag in the core–shell structured catalyst. Ag nanoparticles and carbon dots were served as vehicles and active sites, respectively, and the system could generate free radicals efficiently and continuously. Further, the Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag showed stability and minimal Fe/Ag leakage. The radicals were identified by electron spin resonance spectroscopy and the radical contributions were determined via scavenger experiments. The degradation pathways of penicillin were elucidated.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic drugs have received increasing attention as emerging contaminants owing to their possible threat to the aquatic environment and human health. Most antibiotic drugs have antibacterial function and inhibit the growth of microorganisms [1,2], so they cannot be degraded by biological treatment methods, and can even weaken the biodegradation of other pollutants [3]. Sulfate radical based advanced oxidation processes (SR-AOPs) have emerged as an effective and eco-friendly method for treating pollution in water [4] owing to their high degradation efficacy for recalcitrant pollutants such as antibiotic drugs.

Compared with other free radicals, sulfate radicals have more extensive operating pH range (2–10) and higher redox potential (2.5–3.1 V) [5]. Sodium persulfate (PS), the precursor of sulfate radicals, is a solid crystal with high solubility and stability, and is easy to transport. However, typical self-decomposition of PS is slow and requires activation, so different approaches have been adopted to activate PS for generating free radicals, such as energy activation approaches and addition of transition metals [6,7]. Particularly high requirements of energy and specialized equipment constrained application of energy activation. Various transition elements (e.g. Fe, Cu, Co, and Mo) were used as catalyst and practical treatment in the application of SR-AOPs [8–10]. Iron is widely distributed in the natural environment and has the advantage of being a cost-effective and ecosystem friendly [11,12]. As a heterogeneous material, Fe3O4 has an inverse spinel crystal matrix which was beneficial for electron transfer between Fe(II) and Fe(III) [13]. Fe(II) can initiate PS to form SO4 ·− [14]. However, ion leakage and agglomeration will reduce catalytic activity and need the treatment of iron sludge. In order to address these limitations, composite catalysts are emerging as a research hotspot.

Metal nanoparticles-loaded composite catalysts were widely used in catalysis and optical devices [15-17]. There were many emerging structural forms of them, such as metal–organic frameworks and core–shell structure [18-20]. Core-shell structured composites are composed of substances with different chemical or physical properties in different parts from the core to the outer shell, which means the catalyst can be well encapsulated to reduce ion leakage [21,22]. In contrast to the traditional composite forms, core–shell structure has the advantages of diverse composition, easy adjustment of the material properties, excellent spatial characteristics and synergy, making it an ideal composite material in catalytic applications [23,24]. Owing to their unique structure and properties, core–shell composite nanomaterials have received increasing research attention, and have been widely used in batteries, biological drug delivery, adsorption, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, and chemical catalysis [25]. The key of core–shell structured composites is the efficient transfer of electrons, so vehicles and active sites are needed. As a zero-dimensional semiconductor material, carbon dots (CDs) have excellent photoluminescence and electron transfer/reservoir properties [26] and been proved to be an ideal vehicle which can shuttle the electrons in the composite interface [27]. Ag nanoparticles have thermal and electrical conductivity and are widely used in catalysis [28]. They can serve as active sites on the surface of core–shell structured composites to accelerate electron transfer and improve catalytic efficiency.

In this study, Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag composites with a core–shell structure were successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal method and chemical reduction for activation of PS. They were subsequently studied by multiple characterization methods. The concerted catalysis performance was evaluated based on the penicillin (PEN) degradation efficiency because PEN was a typical antibiotic which was widespread used [29]. Quenching experiments as well as electron spin resonance were used to propose the reaction mechanisms. Based on degradation intermediate analysis, the degradation pathways of penicillin were elucidated.

All chemicals were of analytical grade or higher and used without further purification. Ferrocene, penicillin, ethylenediamine, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine-N-oxide (DMPO), 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and citric acid were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Hydrogen peroxide (30%), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), sodium azide (NaN3), methanol, AgNO3, NaOH, HCl, NaBH4, FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·6H2O, and ammonia solution, were acquired from National Medicines Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HPLC grade acetonitrile, formic acid, and ethanol were obtained from J. T. Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). Sodium persulfate, was bought from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA).

As shown in Fig. 1 , Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag was synthesized using a hydrothermal method and chemical reduction. First, 30% hydrogen peroxide was added to 15 mL of ferrocene solution with vigorous magnetic stirring for 30 min to achieve a uniform mixture. Subsequently, the precursor solution was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless autoclaves, which was then heated to 200 °C and kept at that temperature for 48 h to complete the hydrothermal reaction and magnetic assembly. Thereafter, the material solid was washed with deionized water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C for 18 h to obtain the Fe3O4@C/CDs. The Fe3O4@C/CDs was then dispersed in 10 mL of AgNO3 and vigorously stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, 0.1 mM NaBH4 solution was added for reduction reaction. Finally, the materials were washed with deionized water and ethanol, separated from solution system by a high-strength magnet to obtain Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag. Fe3O4 nanoparticles, CDs, and Ag nanoparticles were synthesized for comparison, the methods are available in the Supplementary material.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were conducted to study the morphology, texture, elemental composition and distribution of the materials. Further, Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to study the composition and structure of the materials. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and magnetic hysteresis loop were used to study the characteristics of the materials. The details are available in the Supplementary material.

The catalytic degradation was evaluated by the degradation of penicillin. The reaction system had a final volume of 100 mL. The reaction was initiated by rapid addition of penicillin, the catalyst, and PS into deionized water. Samples were withdrawn and filtrated by a filter (0.22 μm) and then quenched by methanol. Liquid chromatography (LC-20A, Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to determine the residual penicillin concentration. ICP-MS (NexION 300X, PerkinElme, USA) and Q-TOF-MS (AB Sciex X500R, Framingham, USA) were used to analyze the Fe and Ag ion concentrations and identify the degradation intermediates. Electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments were conducted with a Freiberg MS-5000 instrument (Germany).

The morphology and microstructure of Fe3O4@C/CDs and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag were investigated by the TEM (Fig. 2 ). In Fe3O4@C/CDs (Fig. 2a), Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) with a diameter of 5–20 nm tended to agglomerate and formed multi-cores because of their strong magnetic properties. Amorphous carbon and 3–10 nm CDs encapsulated the Fe3O4 multi-cores and formed a typical core–shell structure with a 20–50 nm shell. After chemical reduction, Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs, 5–10 nm) formed on the shell surface of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag (Fig. 2b). In the HRTEM images of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag (Fig. 2c–d), the d-spacings of the lattice planes were 0.253, 0.335, and 0.236 nm, corresponding to the Fe3O4 (311), C (006), and Ag (111) planes, respectively. The EDX-mapping results of C, Fe, O, and Ag are presented in Fig. 2e. The C atoms were found to be homogeneously distributed, while Fe and O were dispersed in the core section of the nanocomposites. Ag was densely dispersed in the edge region, indicating that it was evenly distributed on the surface of the shell. The EDX spectra of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag showed that the concentrations of Ag and Fe were 13.73% and 3.12%, respectively (Fig. S1). The core–shell structure of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag and the good distribution of Fe3O4, CDs, and Ag would lead to efficient synergy and high catalytic performance.

Fig. 3 a presents the Raman spectra of Fe3O4, CDs, Ag, Fe3O4@C/CDs, and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag. There was a broad band observed in the spectrum of the CDs which indicated the uniform coexistence of amorphous carbon and CDs. The Raman bands at 647 and 364 cm−1 correspond to the A1g and Eg modes of Fe3O4 [30,31]. The feature bands of Ag at 1605, 1316, 1189, and 772 cm−1 were also observed as previous researches [32,33]. The spectra of Fe3O4@C/CDs and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag indicated a distinct D band at approximate 1320–1345 cm−1 attributed to edge defects, and G band at 1550–1600 cm−1 attributed to highly ordered graphite. The feature bands of Fe3O4 and Ag were covered. The D and G bands of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag were less intense than those of Fe3O4@C/CDs, which might be a result of Ag doping. The IG/ID values of Fe3O4@C/CDs and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag were 1.23 and 1.08, respectively, which means that they comprised more crystalline carbon.

The FT-IR spectra of the as-obtained materials were acquired and shown in Fig. 3b to elucidate the functional groups. The spectra of Fe3O4 and CDs had peaks at 570 cm−1 and 2918 cm−1, which were assigned to the Fe-O in Fe3O4 and C-H stretching vibrations in CDs [34]. Both Fe3O4@C/CDs and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag not only showed the same characteristic peaks as Fe3O4 and CDs but also had characteristic peaks at 1441, 1592, 1700, and 3394 cm−1, which associated with the stretching vibrations of C=C, C-C, C=O, and O-H, respectively. Peaks in the spectrum of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag were weaker than those of Fe3O4@C/CDs, which was the result of chemical reduction.

XRD measurements were employed to investigate the structure and composition of the as-prepared materials. Fig. 3c shows the XRD patterns of Fe3O4, CDs, Ag, Fe3O4@C/CDs, and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag. CDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered around 23–25°, which could be assigned to the highly disordered carbon atoms [35]. However, this characteristic diffraction peak was not observed for Fe3O4@C/CDs and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag, which might be attributed to the small amounts and high dispersion of CDs [36]. The patterns of the Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C, and Fe3O4@C-Ag had strong diffraction peaks at 30.1°, 35.4°, 56.9°, and 62.5°, which were attributed to the (220), (311), (511), and (331) plane of Fe3O4, respectively (JCPDS 88–0315). Five sharp peaks were observed in the X-ray diffraction pattern of Ag and Fe3O4@C-Ag located at 38.1°, 44.1°, 64.3°, and 77.18°. These main peaks were assigned to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystalline plane reflections of Ag (JCPDS 04–0783). According to Scherrer equation, Ag NPs had relatively high crystallinity than Fe3O4 NPs because of the sharper characteristic diffraction peaks.

The XPS spectra of survey scan elucidated the elements composition of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag, which were C, O, Ag, and Fe (Fig. 4 a). The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4b) had peaks with binding energies at 530.3, 531.8, and 533.2 eV, which were assigned to lattice oxygen (M-O) in metallic oxide, oxygen-hydrogen bond (O-H), and carbon–oxygen bond (C-O), respectively. Fig. 4c showed four major peaks at 284.8, 285.4, 286.7, and 288.5 eV in the C 1 s spectrum, which corresponded to the carbon bond (C-C), carbon–oxygen bond (C-O), carbon–metal bond (C-M), and sliver-oxygen-carbon bonds (Ag-O-C), respectively. Ag 3d spectrum was deconvolved into 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 spin-orbital components with binding energies at 374.3 and 368.3 eV, which can be indexed as Ag0 (Fig. 4d). The Fe 2p XPS spectrum was deconvolved into Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 states at 711.0 and 724.9 eV, which were characteristics of Fe3O4 [37]. The Fe 3p XPS spectrum was deconvolved into Fe(III) and Fe(II) peaks with an area ratio of 1.94, which also indicated the existence of Fe3O4 (Fig. 4f).

The up-converted PL emission spectra of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag is demonstrated in Fig. 5 a. CDs showed excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior. The excitation light with longer wavelength (500–900 nm) was absorbed by CDs and then emission light with shorter wavelength (200–450 nm) was emit, which proved the existence of CDs [38]. To further demonstrate the interfacial transfer behaviors, EIS of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C/CDs, and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag was detected, and the EIS Nyquist plots in the dark are shown in Fig. 5d. Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag showed the smallest semicircle of the EIS Nyquist plots, which corresponded to the weakest charge resistance (RCT), signifying highest charge transfer and separation efficiency. That could be attributed to the electrical conductivity of CDs and Ag NPs. The results showed that electron could be transferred efficiently.

The magnetic hysteresis loop of different samples at room temperature and maximum field strength of 15 kOe, are shown in Fig. S2. The Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag had a saturation magnetization (Ms) of 21.29 emu/g, a coercivity (Hc) of 299.89 Oe and a remnant magnetization (Mr) of 2.85 emu/g. The Ms and Mr values of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag were lower than those obtained for Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@C/CDs whereas the Hc value was higher. The decreases of the Ms and Mr values were attributed to the presence of amorphous carbon, CDs, and Ag, while the increased Hc value of the composites might be the result of the increase of the surface anisotropy. The specific surface area and porous structure of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag were determined by nitrogen adsorption measurements in Fig. S3. The BET specific surface area of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag was 26.01 m2/g and the pore volume was 0.046 cm3/g. The pore size distribution had three peaks at 1.19 nm, 3.31 nm, and 7.83 nm, which indicates mesoporous structures. The mesoporous structure could serve as molecular diffusion channels to enhance the synergy.

The PEN removal profiles in various systems are shown in Fig. 6 . In the control experiments, PS alone showed slow oxidation of PEN (23.21% within 60 min), suggesting that its direct degradation of PEN was slow. Moreover, Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag alone exhibited a negligible performance, achieved only 3.2% removal of PEN from the aqueous solution in 60 min, which showed that the surface adsorption of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag was poor. As a typical catalyst, Fe3O4 showed a certain catalytic effect. In Fe3O4 activated PS system, 62.4% of PEN was removed. When Fe3O4@C/CDs was tested as catalyst, 100% removal of PEN in 60 min was observed, which indicated the influence of CDs. The electron transfer/reservoir properties of CDs effectively improved the catalytic efficiency. Complete degradation in the Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system was obtained in 30 min, indicating the overwhelming high efficiency of the system, which could be attributed to the synergy of Fe3O4, CDs, and Ag in the core–shell structured catalyst. Further, Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag showed better heterogeneous catalytic performance than other Fe3O4 based catalysts in SR-AOPs system (Table S1.) [39-42].

Experiments comparing different oxidant concentrations and amounts of catalyst are shown in Fig. 7 a-b. The removal efficiencies increased with increasing oxidant concentration and the degradation rate improved at the same time, indicating that the oxidation of PEN through PS activation was strongly affected (Fig. 7a). The PEN could be completely removed in 60 min with the concentration of the oxidant was more than 2.5 mM. A similar trend was observed in investigations that were performed to examine the effect of catalyst dose (Fig. 7a). In the range 0–0.5 g/L, increases in the amount of catalyst resulted in higher degradation rates and efficiencies. When the dosage increased from 0 to 0.05 g/L, a significant improvement in the conversion efficacy was observed. When the catalyst dose was 0.1 g/L, the degradation rate reached 90.1% within 30 min, and complete degradation was achieved within 60 min. Fig. 7c showed the effect of penicillin concentration (1–100 μM). More than 97% removal efficiencies could be achieved in all systems. However, the increase of penicillin concentration had a negative impact on the degradation rate of penicillin which was also observed in other SR-AOPs [43,44]. Fig. 7d showed the effects of pH (3–11) on the Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system. The optimal pH was 3 in which the highest degradation efficiency (100% in 10 min) was achieved. The reaction was inhibited with the increase of pH and only 79.0% of penicillin was degraded at pH 11. Under acidic conditions, additional sulfate radicals could be formed due to the acid-catalysis decomposition of PS [45]. However, the reaction between sulfate radicals and OH– could generate more hydroxyl radicals which had lower redox potential (1.8–2.7 V) than sulfate radicals (2.5–3.1 V). Thus, the system showed weak degradation effect under alkaline conditions.

The reaction temperature was also investigated as a crucial operating parameter in the degradation. PEN degradation was investigated at temperatures in the range 10–50 °C for the Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system. As shown in Fig. S4, the PEN degradation efficiency showed a significant improvement with increasing reaction temperature. At 10 °C, only 31.8% of PEN was removed in 20 min, while complete degradation could be achieved within the same time when the reaction temperature was 50 °C. The kinetic data for the Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS to degrade PEN system were close to first-order as shown in Eq. (1):
(1)
l n (
c t
/
c 0
) = - k t
where k is the rate constant, ct and co are the real-time and initial concentration of PEN, respectively. The k values at the different temperatures had good correlation coefficients (r greater than 0.9455, Table S2) and the rate constants of the reaction increased with increasing reaction temperature (Fig. 8 a). According to the Arrhenius equation:
(2)
l n k = l n A - E a / R T
where k is the rate constant, R is the universal gas constant 8.314 J·mol−1 K−1, and A is a constant. As derived by plotting lnk against 1/T (r = 0.9890), the activation energy (Ea) of the system was 45.25 kJ/mol.

The recyclability of a catalyst is an important factor for its potential application. Fig. 8b shows the recyclability of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag in a five-run test. Recycled catalyst was still highly active after the fifth repeat with the PEN degradation efficiency exceeding 81.3% after 60 min, which indicated the stability of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag. The amount of metal eluting was related to the catalyst loss and evaluated by measuring the concentration of Fe and Ag ions in the solution after each reaction (Fig. S5). After the reaction, the concentrations of Fe and Ag ions decreased from 0.36 mg/L and 0.78 mg/L to 0.26 mg/L and 0.52 mg/L, respectively. Subsequently, Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag was substituted with bare Fe3O4 NPs and Ag NPs to compare the leakage of ions which then showed 13.84 and 28.41 times higher ion concentrations than that of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag system, respectively. The result further indicated the stability and minimal ion leakage of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive species and possible activation mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: ESR technology with DMPO and TEMP utilized as the spin trapping agent to capture the reactive intermediates was demonstrated for the in situ detection of free radicals in the system. The characteristic peaks of DMPO·–OH with hyperfine splitting constants of AN = 14.8 G and AH = 14.8 G could be observed in the PS and Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system in 1 min (Fig. 9 a). The more obvious characteristic peaks in Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system indicating the high-efficiency catalysis. Then a distinct seven peaks signal (AN = 7.3 G and AH = 3.9 G) was observed in 5 min, which could be assigned to the hyperfine splitting of DMPOX. It was the oxidation product of DMPO by highly reactive oxygen-containing species. Because of the different reaction rate of sulfate radicals / DMPO (0.027 M−1s−1) and hydroxyl radicals / DMPO (–1010 M−1s−1), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) was added to avoided the significantly influence of hydroxyl radicals [46]. Finally, signals were detected for DMPO·-SO4 − (AN = 13.8 G, AH = 10.1 G, Aγ1 H = 1.4 G, and Aγ2 H = 0.8 G). The characteristic peaks of DMPO-·O2 − (AN = 12.9 G, AH = 10.2 G) were also observed and showed similar intensity change as DMPO·–OH (Fig. 9b). In Fig. 9c, the free radical trapping with TEMP showed weak TEMPO characteristic peaks (AN = 16.7 G and intensity ratio of 1:1:1), which might be attributed to the singlet-oxygen (1O2) from the self-decomposition of PS. The signals became stronger in Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system. The result indicated that Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag efficiently activated PS to generate free radicals, and SO4 ·−, ·OH, 1O2 and ·O2 − coexisted in the system.

In order to evaluate the contributions of the free radicals, quenching experiments were performed (Fig. 9d). Methanol could quench ·OH and SO4 ·− while TBA could quench only ·OH. The p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) had high reaction rate with ·O2 − (1 × 109 M−1s−1), which could be used to determine the contributions. Sodium azide (NaN3) could quench SO4 ·−, ·OH, and 1O2. To rule out the influence of SO4 ·− and ·OH, excess methanol was also added [47]. The addition of TBA and methanol leaded to degradation efficiencies of 88.8% and 36.6%, respectively. The degradation efficiency had a negligible decrease when p-BQ was added, suggesting the minor contribution of O2 ·−. In the scavenger experiments of 1O2, efficiencies of 33.1% was observed, which were similar to the experiment of methanol alone, suggesting the insignificant roles of them. These results suggested that ·OH and SO4 ·− were key to PEN degradation and the latter had more contribution.

According to the results above and previous research, the reaction mechanism was proposed, which suggested that PS was activated by Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag to generate free radicals for PEN degradation. As represented by Eq. (3), ≡Fe(II) were converted to ≡Fe(III), which catalyze the formation of sulfate radicals from PS. The electrons are transferred to Ag through the carbon dots. Therefore, the Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system could generate free radicals efficiently and continuously (Eqs. (4–7)). The free radicals reacted with PEN and degrade it into intermediate products, and eventually converted it into CO2 and H2O (Eq (8)). The mechanism of Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system for PEN degradation was showed in Fig. 10 .
(3) ≡Fe(II) + S2O8 2− → ≡Fe(III) + SO4 ·− + SO4 2−
(4) SO4 ·− + H2O → H+ + SO4 − + ·OH
(5) SO4 ·− + OH− → SO4 − +·OH
(6) O2 + e− → O2 ·−
(7) ·OH + O2 ·− → OH− + 1O2
(8) PEN + SO4 ·−/ ·OH/ 1O2 /O2 ·− → Oxidation products

The possible reaction pathways (Fig. 11 ) were determined based on the identified intermediates in different time (5 min and 30 min, Table S3 and Fig. S6) and data from previous studies [48-50]. The concentrations of intermediates in the system showed obvious changes in different stage. There were more intermediates with high molecular masses and less small molecule products in 5 min, which changed to opposite trend in 30 min. The first step of the pathways was the breaking of C-C and C-N bonds and the formation of P1–P3 (pathways I–III). In pathway I, P4 and P6 were produced via attacks on C-C in P1 and oxidation reactions. Thereafter, P5 was generated by condensation. In pathway II, P7 was obtained from P2 via the same reaction as for the degradation of P1. Subsequently, the side chain of the benzene ring was attacked to result in P8. In pathway III, P3 was generated by condensation, followed by the generation of P9–10, which were produced by the breaking of C-S and C-N bonds. Finally, the benzene rings were opened and all the intermediates were mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

In this study, Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag composites with a core–shell structure were successfully synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method and chemical reduction for activation of PS and subsequent degradation for PEN. The Fe3O4 multi-cores were encapsulated by a carbon shell (CDs and amorphous carbon) to reduce ion leakage. Ag nanoparticles were immobilized on the surface of the shell. Ag nanoparticles and carbon dots were served as vehicles and active sites, respectively, to accelerate electron transfer and improve catalytic efficiency. As a result, complete PEN degradation was achieved in short time. The degradation efficiency was positively influenced by catalyst dose, oxidant concentration, temperature and low pH. Further, the catalyst exhibited excellent repetitive capacity. Reactive species generated in the system were SO4 ·−, ·OH, 1O2, ·O2 −, and their contributions were evaluated. Therefore, the Fe3O4@C/CDs-Ag activated PS system further extended the application of SR-AOPs in wastewater treatment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (8202029), the National Key R & D Program of China (2018YFD0900805), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U19A20107 and 21307005), the 111 Project (B18006), and Beijing Advanced Innovation Program for Land Surface Science.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2020.117617.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
